sections.label: Production ready
sections.body:
Take a look at the ru.Bee demo site with full documentation: https://rubee.dedyn.io/
Want to explore how it was built? https://github.com/nucleom42/rubee-site

sections.label: Stress tested
sections.body:
CODE>wrk -t4 -c100 -d30s https://rubee.dedyn.io/docs
CODE>Running 30s test @ https://rubee.dedyn.io/docs
CODE>  4 threads and 100 connections
CODE>  Thread Stats   Avg      Stdev     Max   +/- Stdev
CODE>    Latency   304.95ms   33.22ms 551.86ms   90.38%
CODE>    Req/Sec    82.25     42.37   280.00     69.86%
CODE>  9721 requests in 30.02s, 4.11MB read
CODE>Requests/sec:    323.78
CODE>Transfer/sec:    140.07KB

Short output explanation:
  • Requests/sec: ~324
  • Average latency: ~305 ms
  • Total requests handled: 9,721
  • Hardware: Raspberry Pi 5 (8 GB) — single board computer
  • Server: ru.Bee app hosted via Nginx + HTTPS

This demonstrates ru.Bee's efficient architecture and suitability for lightweight deployments — even on low-power hardware.

sections.label: Comparison
sections.body:
Here is a short web frameworks comparison built with Ruby, so you can evaluate your choice with ru.Bee.

Disclaimer:
The comparison is based on generic and subjective information available on the internet and is not a real benchmark. It is aimed at giving you a general idea of the differences between the frameworks and is not intended as a direct comparison.

sections.label: Features
sections.body:
Lightweight – A minimal footprint focused on serving Ruby applications efficiently.

Modular – A modular approach to application development. Build a modular monolith app with ease by attaching as many subprojects as you need.

Contract-driven – Define your API contracts in a simple, declarative way, then generate all the boilerplate you need.

Fast – Optimized for speed, providing quick responses.

Rack-powered – Built on Rack. The full Rack API is available for easy integration.

Databases – Supports SQLite3, PostgreSQL, MySQL, and more via the Sequel gem.

Views – JSON, ERB, and plain HTML out of the box.

React Ready – React is supported as a first-class ru.Bee view engine.

Bundlable – Charge your ru.Bee app with any gem you need. Update effortlessly via Bundler.

ORM-agnostic – Models are native ORM objects, but you can use them as blueprints for any data source.

Authenticatable – Easily add JWT authentication to any controller action.

Hooks – Add logic before, after, or around any controller action.

Testable – Run all or selected tests using fast, beloved Minitest.

Asyncable – Plug in async adapters and use any popular background job engine.

Console – Start an interactive console and reload on the fly.

Background Jobs – Schedule and process background jobs using your preferred async stack.

WebSocket – Serve and handle WebSocket connections.

Logger – Use any logger you want.

sections.label: Installation
sections.body:
1. Install ru.Bee

CODE>gem install ru.Bee

2. Create your first project

CODE>rubee project my_project
CODE>
CODE>cd my_project

3. Install dependencies

Prerequisites: make sure Ruby (3.1 or higher, 3.4.1 recommended) and Bundler are installed.

CODE>bundle install

4. Run the ru.Bee server. Default port is 7000.

CODE>rubee start # or rubee start_dev for development
CODE>
CODE># Starting from version 1.8.0, you can also start the server with the yjit compiler for a speed boost.
CODE>rubee start --jit=yjit
CODE># This option is available for the dev environment too.
CODE>rubee start_dev --jit=yjit

5. Open your browser and go to http://localhost:7000

sections.label: Run tests
sections.body:
CODE>rubee test
CODE># or specify a specific test file
CODE>rubee test models/user_model_test.rb
CODE># or run a specific line in the test file
CODE>rubee test models/user_model_test.rb --line=12

sections.label: Draw contract
sections.body:
1. Add the routes to routes.rb

CODE>    Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>      ...
CODE>      # draw the contract
CODE>      router.get "/apples", to: "apples#index",
CODE>        model: {
CODE>          name: "apple",
CODE>          attributes: [
CODE>            { name: 'id', type: :primary },
CODE>            { name: 'colour', type: :string },
CODE>            { name: 'weight', type: :integer },
CODE>            { name: 'created', type: :datetime },
CODE>            { name: 'updated', type: :datetime },
CODE>          ]
CODE>        }
CODE>    end
CODE>

2. Generate the files

CODE>rubee generate get /apples

This will generate the following files:

CODE>./app/controllers/apples_controller.rb # Controller with respective action
CODE>./app/views/apples_index.erb           # ERB view rendered by the controller
CODE>./app/models/apple.rb                  # Model that acts as ORM
CODE>./db/create_apples.rb                  # Database migration file for the respective table

3. Run the initial database migration

CODE>rubee db run:all

4. Fill the generated files with the logic you need and run the server again.

5. You can find a full snapshot of the schema in the STRUCTURE constant or in the db/structure.rb file.

6. Print the latest schema from the STRUCTURE constant via the CLI

CODE>-> rubee db schema
CODE>--- users
CODE>- id, (PK), type (INTEGER)
CODE>- email, type (varchar(255))
CODE>- password, type (varchar(255))
CODE>
CODE>--- accounts
CODE>- id, (PK), type (INTEGER)
CODE>- address, type (varchar(255))
CODE>- user_id, type (INTEGER)
CODE>
CODE>--- posts
CODE>- id, (PK), type (INTEGER)
CODE>- user_id, type (INTEGER)
CODE>- comment_id, type (INTEGER)
CODE>
CODE>--- comments
CODE>- id, (PK), type (INTEGER)
CODE>- text, type (varchar(255))
CODE>- user_id, type (INTEGER)

7. Print the schema for a specific table

CODE>-> rubee db schema posts
CODE>--- posts
CODE>- id, (PK), type (INTEGER)
CODE>- user_id, type (INTEGER), nullable
CODE>- comment_id, type (INTEGER), nullable
CODE>- created, type (datetime), nullable
CODE>- updated, type (datetime), nullable
CODE>
CODE>  Foreign keys:
CODE>  - comment_id → comments() on delete no_action on update no_action
CODE>  - user_id → users() on delete no_action on update no_action

8. Dropping all tables can be handy during development. Be careful and make sure you pass the desired environment.

CODE>RACK_ENV=test rubee db drop_tables
CODE>These tables have been dropped for the test env:
CODE>[:companies, :company_clients, :services]

sections.label: Model
sections.body:
A model in ru.Bee is a simple Ruby object that can be serialized in the view in whatever form is required (e.g. JSON).
Here is a simple example of rendering JSON from an in-memory object:

CODE># ApplesController
CODE>
CODE>def show
CODE>  # In-memory example
CODE>  apples = [Apple.new(colour: 'red', weight: '1lb'), Apple.new(colour: 'green', weight: '1lb')]
CODE>  apple = apples.find { |apple| apple.colour = params[:colour] }
CODE>
CODE>  response_with object: apple, type: :json
CODE>end

Make sure the Serializable module is included in the target class:

CODE>class Apple
CODE>  include Serializable
CODE>  attr_accessor :id, :colour, :weight
CODE>end

You can also turn it into an ORM object by extending Rubee::SequelObject, which is already serializable and charged with hooks:

CODE>class Apple  user = User.new(email: "llo@ok.com", password: 543)
CODE>=> #

Save a record to the database

CODE>irb(main):018> user.save
CODE>=> true

Update a record with a new value

CODE>irb(main):019> user.update(email: "update@email.com")
CODE>=> #

Check whether a record has been persisted

CODE>irb(main):016> user.persisted?
CODE>=> false

Get a record from the database and reload it

CODE>irb(main):011> user = User.last
CODE>=> #
CODE>irb(main):012> user.email = "new@ok.com"
CODE>=> "new@ok.com"
CODE>irb(main):014> user.reload
CODE>=> # # unpersisted data refreshed from db

Assign attributes without persisting to the database

CODE>irb(main):008> User.last.assign_attributes(email: "bb@ok.com")
CODE>=> {"id" => 2, "email" => "ok23@ok.com", "password" => "123"}

Get all records scoped by a field

CODE>irb(main):005> User.where(email: "ok23@ok.com")
CODE>=> [#]

Get all records

CODE>irb(main):001> User.all
CODE>=> [#]

Find by id

CODE>irb(main):002> user = User.find 1
CODE>=> #

Get the last record

CODE>irb(main):003> User.last
CODE>=> #

Create a new persisted record

CODE>irb(main):004> User.create(email: "ok23@ok.com", password: 123)
CODE>=> #

Destroy a record and all related records

CODE>irb(main):021> user.destroy(cascade: true)
CODE>=> 1

Find a record in the database or initialize a new instance for subsequent persistence

CODE>irb(main):020> user = User.find_or_new(email: "ok23@ok.com")
CODE>=> #
CODE>irb(main):021> user.persisted?
CODE>=> true
CODE>irb(main):022> user = User.find_or_new(email: "new@ok.com")
CODE>=> #
CODE>irb(main):023> user.persisted?
CODE>=> false

Destroy all records one by one

CODE>irb(main):022> User.destroy_all
CODE>=> [#, #]
CODE>irb(main):023> User.all
CODE>=> []

Use complex query chains and serialize results back to ru.Bee objects in a single query:

CODE># user model
CODE>class User  result = Comment.dataset.join(:posts, comment_id: :id)
CODE>irb(main):009>  .where(comment_id: Comment.where(text: "test").last.id)
CODE>irb(main):010>  .then { |dataset| Comment.serialize(dataset) }
CODE>=> [#]

Since version 2.6.0, Rubee::SequelObject supports chained queries. Supported methods: where, order, limit, offset, all, owns_many, owns_one, join, paginate.

CODE>irb(main):001> Comment.where(text: "test").where(user_id: 1)
CODE>=> [#]

A paginate method is also available:

CODE>irb(main):001> comments = Comment.all.paginate(page: 1, per_page: 3)
CODE>
CODE>irb(main):001> comments.pagination_meta
CODE>=> {:current_page=>1, :per_page=>3, :total_count=>10, :first_page=>true, :last_page=>false, :prev=>nil, :next=>2}

sections.label: Model — Rubee::SequelObject base methods
sections.body:
Initiate a new record in memory

CODE>irb(main):015> user = User.new(email: "llo@ok.com", password: 543)
CODE>=> #

Save a record to the database

CODE>irb(main):018> user.save
CODE>=> true

Update a record with a new value

CODE>irb(main):019> user.update(email: "update@email.com")
CODE>=> #

Check whether a record has been persisted

CODE>irb(main):016> user.persisted?
CODE>=> false

Get a record from the database and reload it

CODE>irb(main):011> user = User.last
CODE>=> #
CODE>irb(main):012> user.email = "new@ok.com"
CODE>=> "new@ok.com"
CODE>irb(main):014> user.reload
CODE>=> # # unpersisted data refreshed from db

Assign attributes without persisting to the database

CODE>irb(main):008> User.last.assign_attributes(email: "bb@ok.com")
CODE>=> {"id" => 2, "email" => "ok23@ok.com", "password" => "123"}

Get all records scoped by a field

CODE>irb(main):005> User.where(email: "ok23@ok.com")
CODE>=> [#]

Get all records

CODE>irb(main):001> User.all
CODE>=> [#]

Find by id

CODE>irb(main):002> user = User.find 1
CODE>=> #

Get the last record

CODE>irb(main):003> User.last
CODE>=> #

Create a new persisted record

CODE>irb(main):004> User.create(email: "ok23@ok.com", password: 123)
CODE>=> #

Destroy a record and all related records

CODE>irb(main):021> user.destroy(cascade: true)
CODE>=> 1

Find a record in the database or initialize a new instance for subsequent persistence

CODE>irb(main):020> user = User.find_or_new(email: "ok23@ok.com")
CODE>=> #
CODE>irb(main):021> user.persisted?
CODE>=> true
CODE>irb(main):022> user = User.find_or_new(email: "new@ok.com")
CODE>=> #
CODE>irb(main):023> user.persisted?
CODE>=> false

Destroy all records one by one

CODE>irb(main):022> User.destroy_all
CODE>=> [#, #]
CODE>irb(main):023> User.all
CODE>=> []

Use complex query chains and serialize results back to ru.Bee objects in a single query:

CODE># user model
CODE>class User  result = Comment.dataset.join(:posts, comment_id: :id)
CODE>irb(main):009>  .where(comment_id: Comment.where(text: "test").last.id)
CODE>irb(main):010>  .then { |dataset| Comment.serialize(dataset) }
CODE>=> [#]

Since version 2.6.0, Rubee::SequelObject supports chained queries. Supported methods: where, order, limit, offset, all, owns_many, owns_one, join, paginate.

CODE>irb(main):001> Comment.where(text: "test").where(user_id: 1)
CODE>=> [#]

A paginate method is also available:

CODE>irb(main):001> comments = Comment.all.paginate(page: 1, per_page: 3)
CODE>
CODE>irb(main):001> comments.pagination_meta
CODE>=> {:current_page=>1, :per_page=>3, :total_count=>10, :first_page=>true, :last_page=>false, :prev=>nil, :next=>2}

sections.label: Database
sections.body:
ru.Bee supports Postgres and SQLite databases fully and can potentially be used with any database supported by the Sequel gem.

When using SQLite, include sqlite3 in your Gemfile:

CODE>gem 'sqlite3'

Define your database URLs for each environment in config/base_configuration.rb:

CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :development) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: 'sqlite://db/development.db', env: }
CODE>  ...
CODE>end
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :test) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: 'sqlite://db/test.db', env: }
CODE>  ...
CODE>end
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :production) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: 'sqlite://db/production.db', env: }
CODE>  ...
CODE>end

For PostgreSQL, include the pg gem and configure the URLs:

CODE>gem 'pg'

CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :development) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: "postgres://postgres@localhost:5432/development", env: }
CODE>  ...
CODE>end
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :test) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: "postgres://postgres@localhost:5432/test", env: }
CODE>  ...
CODE>end
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :production) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: "postgres://postgres:#{ENV['DB_PASSWORD']}@localhost:5432/production", env: }
CODE>  ...
CODE>end

Before starting the server or running the test suite, ensure your database is initialized:

CODE>rubee db init                         # ensures your database is created for each environment
CODE>RACK_ENV=test rubee db run:all        # runs all migrations for the test environment
CODE>RACK_ENV=development rubee db run:all # runs all migrations for the development environment

SQLite production ready

Starting from version 1.9.0, the main issue with SQLite — write database locking — is resolved.
You can tune the retry configuration parameters as needed:

```ruby

sections.label: Database — SQLite production ready
sections.body:
Starting from version 1.9.0, the main issue with SQLite — write database locking — is resolved.
You can tune the retry configuration parameters as needed:

```ruby

sections.label: configure database write retries
sections.body:
config.db_max_retries    = { env:, value: 3 }     # set to 0 to disable, or increase if needed
config.db_retry_delay    = { env:, value: 0.1 }
config.db_busy_timeout   = { env:, value: 1000 }  # busy timeout in milliseconds before raising an error

CODE>
CODE>For ru.Bee model create and update methods, retries are added automatically. To use retries with a Sequel dataset directly:
CODE>
ruby
Rubee::DBTools.with_retry { User.dataset.insert(email: "test@ok.com", password: "123") }
```

sections.label: Routing
sections.body:
ru.Bee uses explicit routes. In routes.rb you can define routes for any of the main HTTP methods.
You can also include matched parameters denoted by { } in the route path, e.g. /path/to/{a_key}/somewhere.

Routing methods

CODE>Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>  router.get     '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.post    '/posts',       to: 'posts#create'
CODE>  router.patch   '/posts/{id}',  to: 'posts#update'
CODE>  router.put     '/posts/{id}',  to: 'posts#update'
CODE>  router.delete  '/posts/{id}',  to: 'posts#delete'
CODE>  router.head    '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.connect '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.options '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.trace   '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>end

Every route follows this structure:

CODE>route.{http_method} {path}, to: "{controller}#{action}",
CODE>  model: { ...optional }, namespace: { ...optional }, react: { ...optional }

Defining model attributes in routes

One of ru.Bee's unique traits is defining models for generation directly in the routes:

CODE>Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>  ...
CODE>  router.get "/apples", to: "apples#index",
CODE>    model: {
CODE>      name: "apple",
CODE>      attributes: [
CODE>        { name: 'id', type: :primary },
CODE>        { name: 'colour', type: :string },
CODE>        { name: 'weight', type: :integer },
CODE>        { name: 'created', type: :datetime },
CODE>        { name: 'updated', type: :datetime },
CODE>      ]
CODE>    }
CODE>end

Other supported attribute types via Sequel:

CODE>[
CODE>  { name: 'id',              type: :primary },
CODE>  { name: 'name',            type: :string },
CODE>  { name: 'description',     type: :text },
CODE>  { name: 'quantity',        type: :integer },
CODE>  { name: 'created',         type: :date },
CODE>  { name: 'modified',        type: :datetime },
CODE>  { name: 'exists',          type: :time },
CODE>  { name: 'active',          type: :boolean },
CODE>  { name: 'hash',            type: :bigint },
CODE>  { name: 'price',           type: :decimal },
CODE>  { name: 'item_id',         type: :foreign_key },
CODE>  { name: 'item_id_index',   type: :index },
CODE>  { name: 'item_id_unique',  type: :unique }
CODE>]

Every attribute can carry options based on the Sequel schema definition. For example:

CODE>{ name: 'key', type: :string, options: { size: 50, fixed: true } }

Gets translated to:

CODE>String :key, size: 50, fixed: true

Generation from routes

As long as a route has a model: key, you can use it to generate initial model files. If only path and to: are defined, only a controller and view will be generated.

CODE>rubee generate get /apples           # or: rubee gen get /apples
CODE>rubee generate patch /apples/{id}    # or: rubee gen patch /apples/{id}

Example 1 — route without a model:

CODE>router.get "/apples", to: "apples#index"

Generates:

CODE>./app/controllers/apples_controller.rb
CODE>./app/views/apples_index.erb

Example 2 — route with a model name only:

CODE>router.get "/apples", to: "apples#index", model: { name: 'apple' }

Generates:

CODE>./app/controllers/apples_controller.rb
CODE>./app/views/apples_index.erb
CODE>./app/models/apple.rb
CODE>./db/create_apples.rb

Example 3 — route with full model attributes:

CODE>router.get "/apples", to: "apples#index",
CODE>  model: {
CODE>    name: 'apple',
CODE>    attributes: [
CODE>      { name: 'id', type: :primary },
CODE>      { name: 'colour', type: :string },
CODE>      { name: 'weight', type: :integer },
CODE>      { name: 'created', type: :datetime },
CODE>      { name: 'updated', type: :datetime },
CODE>    ]
CODE>  }

Generates:

CODE>./app/controllers/apples_controller.rb
CODE>./app/models/apple.rb
CODE>./app/views/apples_index.erb
CODE>./db/create_apples.rb

Modular application

ru.Bee supports modular applications — attach as many subprojects as you need. Each subproject gets its own folder, MVC setup, routes, and namespacing, while still sharing data with the main app.

1. Attach a new subproject

CODE>rubee attach admin

2. Add routes

CODE># admin_routes.rb
CODE>Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>  router.get '/admin/cabbages', to: 'cabbages#index',
CODE>                               model: {
CODE>                                 name: 'cabbage',
CODE>                                 attributes: [
CODE>                                   { name: 'id', type: :primary },
CODE>                                   { name: 'name', type: :string },
CODE>                                   { name: 'created', type: :datetime },
CODE>                                   { name: 'updated', type: :datetime },
CODE>                                 ]
CODE>                               },
CODE>                               namespace: :admin  # mandatory for namespacing support
CODE>end

3. Run the generate command

CODE>rubee gen get /admin/cabbages app:admin

Generates:

CODE>./admin/controllers/cabbages_controller.rb
CODE>./admin/views/cabbages_index.erb
CODE>./admin/models/cabbage.rb
CODE>./db/create_cabbages.rb

4. Run the migration

CODE>rubee db run:create_cabbages

5. Fill the controller with content

CODE># ./admin/controllers/cabbages_controller.rb
CODE>class Admin::CabbagesController < Rubee::BaseController
CODE>  def index
CODE>    response_with object: Cabbage.all, type: :json
CODE>  end
CODE>end

6. Run the server

CODE>rubee start  # or rubee start_dev for development

sections.label: Routing — Routing methods
sections.body:
CODE>Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>  router.get     '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.post    '/posts',       to: 'posts#create'
CODE>  router.patch   '/posts/{id}',  to: 'posts#update'
CODE>  router.put     '/posts/{id}',  to: 'posts#update'
CODE>  router.delete  '/posts/{id}',  to: 'posts#delete'
CODE>  router.head    '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.connect '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.options '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>  router.trace   '/posts',       to: 'posts#index'
CODE>end

Every route follows this structure:

CODE>route.{http_method} {path}, to: "{controller}#{action}",
CODE>  model: { ...optional }, namespace: { ...optional }, react: { ...optional }

CODE>./db/create_apples.rb

sections.label: Routing — Modular application
sections.body:
ru.Bee supports modular applications — attach as many subprojects as you need. Each subproject gets its own folder, MVC setup, routes, and namespacing, while still sharing data with the main app.

1. Attach a new subproject

CODE>rubee attach admin

2. Add routes

CODE># admin_routes.rb
CODE>Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>  router.get '/admin/cabbages', to: 'cabbages#index',
CODE>                               model: {
CODE>                                 name: 'cabbage',
CODE>                                 attributes: [
CODE>                                   { name: 'id', type: :primary },
CODE>                                   { name: 'name', type: :string },
CODE>                                   { name: 'created', type: :datetime },
CODE>                                   { name: 'updated', type: :datetime },
CODE>                                 ]
CODE>                               },
CODE>                               namespace: :admin  # mandatory for namespacing support
CODE>end

3. Run the generate command

CODE>rubee gen get /admin/cabbages app:admin

Generates:

CODE>./admin/controllers/cabbages_controller.rb
CODE>./admin/views/cabbages_index.erb
CODE>./admin/models/cabbage.rb
CODE>./db/create_cabbages.rb

4. Run the migration

CODE>rubee db run:create_cabbages

5. Fill the controller with content

CODE># ./admin/controllers/cabbages_controller.rb
CODE>class Admin::CabbagesController < Rubee::BaseController
CODE>  def index
CODE>    response_with object: Cabbage.all, type: :json
CODE>  end
CODE>end

6. Run the server

CODE>rubee start  # or rubee start_dev for development

sections.label: Views
sections.body:
A view in ru.Bee is a plain HTML, ERB, or React file rendered from the controller.

Templates with ERB

layout.erb is the parent template rendered first; child templates are rendered inside it. Feel free to include custom CSS and JS files there.

CODE># app/controllers/welcome_controller.rb
CODE>
CODE>class WelcomeController
CODE>
CODE>All set up and running!

CODE>
CODE>
CODE>
CODE>
CODE>
CODE>

React as a view

React is supported out of the box as a view layer in ru.Bee.

Prerequisites: Node and NPM are required.

1. After creating your project and bundling, install React dependencies:

CODE>rubee react prepare

2. Configure React in config/base_configuration.rb:

CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :development) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: 'sqlite://db/development.db', env: }
CODE>
CODE>  # register React as a view
CODE>  config.react = { on: true, env: }
CODE>end

3. Start the server:

CODE>rubee start
CODE># Default port is 7000. To change it:
CODE>rubee start --port=3000

4. Open your browser and navigate to http://localhost:3000/home.

5. For development, run rubee start_dev in one terminal and rubee react watch in another. Changes apply instantly.

6. In production, rebuild the React app with rubee react build. Not needed in development when using rubee react watch.

7. Generate a React view from a route by specifying the view name:

CODE># config/routes.rb
CODE>Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>  router.get('/', to: 'welcome#show')
CODE>
CODE>  router.get('/api/users', to: 'user#index', react: { view_name: 'users.tsx' })
CODE>  # Note: /api/users is the backend endpoint.
CODE>  # To render /app/views/users.tsx, update the React routes as shown below.
CODE>end

8. Add logic to the generated API controller:

CODE># app/controllers/api/user_controller.rb
CODE>class Api::UserController
CODE>
CODE>    } />
CODE>    } />
CODE>
CODE>

10. Fetch data from the backend in the component:

CODE>// app/views/users.tsx
CODE>import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
CODE>
CODE>function Users() {
CODE>  const [users, setUsers] = useState([]);
CODE>
CODE>  useEffect(() => {
CODE>    fetch('/api/users')
CODE>      .then(response => response.json())
CODE>      .then(data => setUsers(data));
CODE>  }, []);
CODE>
CODE>  return (
CODE>
CODE>      Users
CODE>
CODE>        {users.map(user => (
CODE>          id: {user.id}: {user.name}
CODE>        ))}
CODE>
CODE>
CODE>  );
CODE>}

sections.label: Views — Templates with ERB
sections.body:
layout.erb is the parent template rendered first; child templates are rendered inside it. Feel free to include custom CSS and JS files there.

CODE># app/controllers/welcome_controller.rb
CODE>
CODE>class WelcomeController
CODE>
CODE>All set up and running!

CODE>
CODE>
CODE>
CODE>
CODE>
CODE>

CODE>end

8. Add logic to the generated API controller:

CODE>    } />
CODE>    } />
CODE>
CODE>

10. Fetch data from the backend in the component:

CODE>// app/views/users.tsx
CODE>import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
CODE>
CODE>function Users() {
CODE>  const [users, setUsers] = useState([]);
CODE>
CODE>  useEffect(() => {
CODE>    fetch('/api/users')
CODE>      .then(response => response.json())
CODE>      .then(data => setUsers(data));
CODE>  }, []);
CODE>

CODE>        ))}

sections.label: Object hooks
sections.body:
By including the Hookable module, any Ruby object can be charged with hooks — logic that executes before, after, or around a specific method call.

BaseController is Hookable by default:

CODE>class ApplesController  { puts "after index" },  if:     -> { true }   # or use a lambda
CODE>  after  :index, -> { puts "after index2" }, unless: -> { false }  # if/unless guards accept a method or lambda
CODE>  around :index, :log
CODE>
CODE>  def index
CODE>    response_with object: { test: "hooks" }
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  def print_hello
CODE>    puts "hello!"
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  def log
CODE>    puts "before log around"
CODE>    res = yield
CODE>    puts "after log around"
CODE>    res
CODE>  end
CODE>end

The server logs will show the following execution stack:

CODE>before log around
CODE>hello!
CODE>after index
CODE>after index2
CODE>after log around
CODE>127.0.0.1 - - [17/Feb/2025:11:42:14 -0500] "GET /apples HTTP/1.1" 401 - 0.0359

Starting from version 1.11, hooks can also be pinned to class methods:

CODE>class AnyClass
CODE>  include Rubee::Hookable
CODE>  before :print_world, :print_hello, class_methods: true
CODE>
CODE>  class << self
CODE>    def print_world
CODE>      puts "world!"
CODE>    end
CODE>
CODE>    def print_hello
CODE>      puts "hello!"
CODE>    end
CODE>  end
CODE>end

Output:

CODE>hello!
CODE>world!

sections.label: Validations
sections.body:
Any class can be charged with validations by including the Validatable module.
ru.Bee models are validatable by default — no need to include it explicitly.

CODE>class Foo
CODE>  include Rubee::Validatable
CODE>
CODE>  attr_accessor :name, :age
CODE>
CODE>  def initialize(name, age)
CODE>    @name = name
CODE>    @age = age
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  validate do
CODE>    attribute(:name).required.type(String).condition(->{ name.length > 2 })
CODE>
CODE>    attribute(:age)
CODE>      .required('Age is a mandatory field')
CODE>      .type(Integer, error_message: 'Must be an integer!')
CODE>      .condition(->{ age > 18 }, fancy_error: 'You must be at least 18 years old!')
CODE>  end
CODE>end

CODE>irb(main):041> Foo.new("Test", 20).valid?
CODE>=> true
CODE>irb(main):042> Foo.new("Test", 1).errors
CODE>=> {age: {fancy_error: "You must be at least 18 years old!"}}
CODE>irb(main):046> Foo.new("Joe", "wrong").valid?
CODE>=> false
CODE>irb(main):047> Foo.new("Joe", "wrong").errors
CODE>=> {age: {error_message: "Must be an integer!"}}

Model example with persistence guards:

CODE>class User { email.match?(/\A[\w+\-.]+@[a-z\d\-]+(\.[a-z\d\-]+)*\.[a-z]+\z/i) }, error: 'Wrong email format'
CODE>      )
CODE>  end
CODE>end

CODE>irb(main):077> user.save
CODE>=> {email: {error: "Wrong email format"}} (Rubee::Validatable::Error)
CODE>irb(main):078> user.email = "ok@ok.com"
CODE>irb(main):080> user.save
CODE>=> true

To apply validate_before_persist! and validate_after_setters globally, add an initializer such as init/sequel_object_preloader.rb:

CODE>Rubee::SequelObject.validate_before_persist!
CODE>Rubee::SequelObject.validate_after_setters

sections.label: Rubee support
sections.body:
An optional set of useful methods can be added to base Ruby classes globally via configuration:

CODE># Include all support methods
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup do |config|
CODE>  config.rubee_support = { all: true }
CODE>end
CODE>
CODE># Include only methods for a specific class
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup do |config|
CODE>  config.rubee_support = { classes: [Rubee::Support::String] }
CODE>end

Available extensions:

CODE># Hash — tolerates string or symbol keys interchangeably
CODE>{one: 1}[:one]   # => 1
CODE>{one: 1}["one"]  # => 1
CODE>
CODE># Hash — deep digging
CODE>{one: {two: 2}}.deep_dig(:two)  # => 2

CODE># String — enriched with helper methods
CODE>"test".pluralize   # => "tests"
CODE>"test".singularize # => "test"
CODE>"test".camelize    # => "Test"
CODE>"TestMe".snakeize  # => "test_me"
CODE>"test".singular?   # => true
CODE>"test".plural?     # => false

sections.label: JWT based authentication
sections.body:
Include the AuthTokenable module in your controller and authenticate any action you need.

First, initialize the User model:

CODE>rubee db run:create_users

This creates the users table and seeds it with demo credentials — email ok@ok.com, password password. Customize /db/create_users.rb before running the migration if needed.

CODE>class UsersController < Rubee::BaseController
CODE>  include Rubee::AuthTokenable
CODE>  auth_methods :index  # unauthenticated requests to these actions will be rejected
CODE>
CODE>  # GET /users/login
CODE>  def edit
CODE>    response_with
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # POST /users/login
CODE>  def login
CODE>    if authenticate!  # initializes @token_header
CODE>      response_with type: :redirect, to: "/users", headers: @token_header
CODE>    else
CODE>      @error = "Wrong email or password"
CODE>      response_with render_view: "users_edit"
CODE>    end
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # POST /users/logout
CODE>  def logout
CODE>    unauthenticate!
CODE>    response_with type: :redirect, to: "/users/login", headers: @zeroed_token_header
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # GET /users (restricted)
CODE>  def index
CODE>    response_with object: User.all, type: :json
CODE>  end
CODE>end

Set a JWT_KEY at startup for security:

CODE>JWT_KEY=SDJwer0wer23j rubee start

To use a custom model instead of the default User, pass arguments to authenticate! and unauthenticate!:

CODE>if authenticate! user_model: Client, login: :name, password: :digest_password
CODE>  response_with type: :redirect, to: "/clients", headers: @token_header
CODE>end

sections.label: OAuth authentication
sections.body:
To plug in OAuth 2.0 authentication, add the oauth2 gem to your Gemfile:

CODE>gem 'oauth2'

Use the following as a starting point:

CODE>class UsersController < Rubee::BaseController
CODE>  include Rubee::AuthTokenable
CODE>
CODE>  REDIRECT_URI  = 'https://mysite.com/users/oauth_callback'
CODE>  CLIENT_ID     = ENV['GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID']
CODE>  CLIENT_SECRET = ENV['GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET']
CODE>
CODE>  # GET /login
CODE>  def edit
CODE>    response_with
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # POST /users/login
CODE>  def login
CODE>    if authenticate!
CODE>      response_with(type: :redirect, to: "/sections", headers: @token_header)
CODE>    else
CODE>      @error = "Wrong email or password"
CODE>      response_with(render_view: "users_edit")
CODE>    end
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # GET /users/oauth_login
CODE>  def oauth_login
CODE>    response_with(
CODE>      type: :redirect,
CODE>      to: auth_client.auth_code.authorize_url(
CODE>        redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI,
CODE>        scope: 'email profile openid'
CODE>      )
CODE>    )
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # GET /users/oauth_callback
CODE>  def oauth_callback
CODE>    code      = params[:code]
CODE>    token     = auth_client.auth_code.get_token(code, redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI)
CODE>    user_info = JSON.parse(token.get('https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v1/userinfo?alt=json').body)
CODE>
CODE>    user = User.where(email: user_info['email'])&.last
CODE>    raise "User with email #{user_info['email']} not found" unless user
CODE>
CODE>    params[:email]    = user_info['email']
CODE>    params[:password] = user.password
CODE>
CODE>    if authenticate!
CODE>      response_with(type: :redirect, to: "/sections", headers: @token_header)
CODE>    else
CODE>      @error = "Something went wrong"
CODE>      response_with(render_view: "users_edit")
CODE>    end
CODE>  rescue OAuth2::Error
CODE>    @error = "OAuth login failed"
CODE>    response_with(render_view: "users_edit")
CODE>  rescue StandardError
CODE>    @error = "Something went wrong"
CODE>    response_with(render_view: "users_edit")
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # POST /users/logout
CODE>  def logout
CODE>    unauthenticate!
CODE>    response_with(type: :redirect, to: "/login", headers: @zeroed_token_header)
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  private
CODE>
CODE>  def auth_client
CODE>    @client ||= OAuth2::Client.new(
CODE>      CLIENT_ID,
CODE>      CLIENT_SECRET,
CODE>      site:          'https://accounts.google.com',
CODE>      authorize_url: '/o/oauth2/auth',
CODE>      token_url:     'https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token'
CODE>    )
CODE>  end
CODE>end

sections.label: ru.Bee commands
sections.body:
CODE>rubee start          # start the server
CODE>rubee start_dev      # start the server in dev mode, restarting on file changes
CODE>rubee react prepare  # install React dependencies
CODE>rubee react watch    # React dev mode, use together with start_dev
CODE>rubee stop           # stop the server
CODE>rubee restart        # restart the server

sections.label: Generate commands
sections.body:
CODE>rubee generate get /apples  # generate controller, view, model, and migration if set in routes
CODE>rubee gen get /apples        # shorthand alias

sections.label: Migration commands
sections.body:
CODE>rubee db run:all              # run all migrations
CODE>rubee db run:create_apples    # run a specific migration file from /db
CODE>rubee db structure            # generate a migration file for the database structure

sections.label: ru.Bee console
sections.body:
CODE>rubee console  # start the interactive console
CODE># type 'reload' inside the console to pick up the latest changes

To run any ru.Bee command in a specific environment, prefix with the env variable:

CODE>RACK_ENV=test rubee console

sections.label: Testing
sections.body:
CODE>rubee test                                          # run all tests
CODE>rubee test auth_tokenable_test.rb                   # run a specific test file
CODE>rubee test models/user_model_test.rb --line=12      # run a specific line

sections.label: Background jobs
sections.body:
Sidekiq engine

1. Add Sidekiq to your Gemfile

CODE>gem 'sidekiq'

2. Configure the adapter for the desired environment

CODE># config/base_configuration.rb
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :development) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url  = { url: "sqlite://db/development.db", env: }
CODE>  config.async_adapter = { async_adapter: SidekiqAsync, env: }
CODE>end

3. Install dependencies

CODE>bundle install

4. Start Redis

CODE>redis-server

5. Add a Sidekiq configuration file

CODE># config/sidekiq.yml
CODE>
CODE>development:
CODE>  redis: redis://localhost:6379/0
CODE>  concurrency: 5
CODE>  queues:
CODE>    default:
CODE>    low:
CODE>    high:

6. Create a Sidekiq worker

CODE># app/async/test_async_runner.rb
CODE>require_relative 'extensions/asyncable' unless defined? Asyncable
CODE>
CODE>class TestAsyncRunner
CODE>  include Rubee::Asyncable
CODE>  include Sidekiq::Worker
CODE>
CODE>  sidekiq_options queue: :default
CODE>
CODE>  def perform(options)
CODE>    User.create(email: options['email'], password: options['password'])
CODE>  end
CODE>end

7. Use it in your codebase

CODE>TestAsyncRunner.new.perform_async(options: { "email" => "new@new.com", "password" => "123" })

Default engine — ThreadAsync

The default adapter is ThreadAsync. It is not yet recommended for production — use with caution.

1. Do not define any adapter in config/base_configuration.rb; the default ThreadAsync will be used.
2. Create a worker and process it:

CODE># test_async_runner.rb
CODE>class TestAsyncRunner
CODE>  include Rubee::Asyncable
CODE>
CODE>  def perform(options)
CODE>    User.create(email: options['email'], password: options['password'])
CODE>  end
CODE>end
CODE>
CODE>TestAsyncRunner.new.perform_async(options: { "email" => "new@new.com", "password" => "123" })

sections.label: Background jobs — Sidekiq engine
sections.body:
1. Add Sidekiq to your Gemfile

2. Configure the adapter for the desired environment

sections.label: Background jobs — Default engine — ThreadAsync
sections.body:
The default adapter is ThreadAsync. It is not yet recommended for production — use with caution.

1. Do not define any adapter in config/base_configuration.rb; the default ThreadAsync will be used.
2. Create a worker and process it:

CODE># test_async_runner.rb
CODE>class TestAsyncRunner
CODE>  include Rubee::Asyncable

CODE>  def perform(options)
CODE>    User.create(email: options['email'], password: options['password'])
CODE>  end
CODE>end
CODE>

sections.label: Logger
sections.body:
Use your own logger by setting it in config/base_configuration.rb:

CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :development) do |config|
CODE>  config.database_url = { url: "sqlite://db/development.db", env: }
CODE>  config.logger       = { logger: MyLogger, env: }
CODE>end

Or use the built-in logger with its full set of levels:

CODE># app/controllers/welcome_controller.rb
CODE>class WelcomeController (&target_method) do
CODE>    start = Time.now
CODE>    Rubee::Logger.warn(message: 'This is a warning message', method: :show, class_name: 'WelcomeController')
CODE>    Rubee::Logger.error(message: 'This is an error message', class_name: 'WelcomeController')
CODE>    Rubee::Logger.critical(message: 'We are on fire!')
CODE>    target_method.call
CODE>    Rubee::Logger.info(
CODE>      message: "Execution Time: #{Time.now - start} seconds",
CODE>      method: :show,
CODE>      class_name: 'WelcomeController'
CODE>    )
CODE>    Rubee::Logger.debug(object: User.last, method: :show, class_name: 'WelcomeController')
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  def show
CODE>    response_with
CODE>  end
CODE>end

Output:

CODE>[2025-04-26 12:32:33] WARN     [method: show][class_name: WelcomeController] This is a warning message
CODE>[2025-04-26 12:32:33] ERROR    [class_name: WelcomeController] This is an error message
CODE>[2025-04-26 12:32:33] CRITICAL We are on fire!
CODE>[2025-04-26 12:32:33] INFO     [method: show][class_name: WelcomeController] Execution Time: 0.000655 seconds
CODE>[2025-04-26 12:32:33] DEBUG    [method: show][class_name: WelcomeController] #

sections.label: WebSocket
sections.body:
With ru.Bee 2.0.0 you can use WebSocket with ease.

1. Install and start Redis

CODE>sudo apt-get install -y redis  # Linux
CODE>brew install redis              # macOS

2. Add the required gems to your Gemfile

CODE>gem 'ru.Bee'
CODE>gem 'redis'
CODE>gem 'websocket'

3. Add the Redis URL to your configuration, unless it defaults to 127.0.0.1:6379

CODE># config/base_configuration.rb
CODE>Rubee::Configuration.setup(env = :development) do |config|
CODE>  ...
CODE>  config.redis_url = { url: "redis://localhost:6378/0", env: }
CODE>end

4. Add a WebSocket entry route

CODE># config/routes.rb
CODE>Rubee::Router.draw do |router|
CODE>  ...
CODE>  router.get('/ws', to: 'users#websocket')
CODE>  # On the client: const ws = new WebSocket("ws://website/ws");
CODE>end

5. Make the model pub/sub capable

CODE># app/models/user.rb
CODE>class User  e
CODE>    response_with(object: { type: 'system', error: e.message }, type: :websocket)
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # Expected client params: { action: 'unsubscribe', channel: 'default', id: '123', subscriber: 'User' }
CODE>  def unsubscribe
CODE>    channel   = params[:channel]
CODE>    sender_id = params[:options][:id]
CODE>    io        = params[:options][:io]
CODE>
CODE>    User.unsub(channel, sender_id, io) do |channel, args|
CODE>      websocket_connections.remove(channel, args[:io])
CODE>    end
CODE>    response_with(object: params.merge(type: 'system', status: :unsubscribed), type: :websocket)
CODE>  rescue StandardError => e
CODE>    response_with(object: { type: 'system', error: e.message }, type: :websocket)
CODE>  end
CODE>
CODE>  # Expected client params: { action: 'publish', channel: 'default', message: 'Hello', id: '123', subscriber: 'User' }
CODE>  def publish
CODE>    args = {}
CODE>    User.pub(params[:channel], message: params[:message]) do |channel|
CODE>      user               = User.find(params[:options][:id])
CODE>      args[:message]     = params[:message]
CODE>      args[:sender]      = params[:options][:id]
CODE>      args[:sender_name] = user.email
CODE>      websocket_connections.stream(channel, args)
CODE>    end
CODE>    response_with(object: { type: 'system', message: params[:message], status: :published }, type: :websocket)
CODE>  rescue StandardError => e
CODE>    response_with(object: { type: 'system', error: e.message }, type: :websocket)
CODE>  end
CODE>end

For a full chat application example, see rubee-chat.

sections.label: Bee assistant
sections.body:
ru.Bee ships with a built-in CLI assistant called bee. It answers questions about the framework directly in your terminal, using a local TF-IDF knowledge base built from the project documentation. Optionally, it routes answers through a local Ollama language model for richer, more conversational responses.

No external API keys or internet connection are required in the default mode.

Building the knowledge base

Before using the assistant for the first time, generate the knowledge base from the README:

CODE>rubee bee generate  # or: rubee bee gen

This parses the documentation, computes TF-IDF vectors, and writes a bee_knowledge.json file to lib/rubee/cli/. Re-run this command any time the documentation is updated.

Interactive mode

Start an interactive session and ask questions conversationally:

CODE>rubee bee

CODE>  ⬡ ⬢ ⬢  ru.Bee — domestic AI assistant
CODE>  ──────────────────────────────────────────────
CODE>  Ask me anything about the ru.Bee framework.
CODE>  Type exit to leave  •  rubee bee generate to retrain.
CODE>  You: How do I run the server?

Type exit, quit, bye, or q to leave the session.

Single-shot mode

Pass a question directly as a command-line argument to get one answer and exit:

CODE>rubee bee how do hooks work
CODE>rubee bee what databases are supported
CODE>rubee bee how do I set up JWT authentication

LLM mode

If you have Ollama installed and running locally, enable LLM mode for more detailed answers. The assistant retrieves the most relevant documentation and passes it as context to the model.

CODE>rubee bee --llm                        # interactive mode, default model (qwen2.5:1.5b)
CODE>rubee bee --llm=llama3.2               # interactive mode, specific model
CODE>rubee bee --llm how do hooks work      # single-shot LLM answer
CODE>rubee bee --llm=qwen2.5:0.5b how do I configure WebSocket  # single-shot with specific model

If the specified model is not available locally, the assistant automatically pulls it from Ollama before answering, displaying a live download progress bar.

Environment options

CODE>OLLAMA_URL=http://remote-host:11434 rubee bee --llm  # use a custom Ollama endpoint
CODE>BEE_KNOWLEDGE=/path/to/custom.json rubee bee         # use a custom knowledge base file
CODE>BEE_DEBUG=1 rubee bee --llm                          # write LLM debug output to /tmp/bee_ollama_debug.txt

Suggestions

After every answer, the assistant suggests up to five related topics you might want to explore next, along with a link to the full documentation at https://rubee.dedyn.io/.

Command reference

CODE>rubee bee generate                      # build the knowledge base from the README
CODE>rubee bee gen                           # alias for generate
CODE>rubee bee                               # start interactive mode
CODE>rubee bee                     # single-shot answer
CODE>rubee bee --llm                         # interactive LLM mode (default model: qwen2.5:1.5b)
CODE>rubee bee --llm=                 # interactive LLM mode with a specific Ollama model
CODE>rubee bee --llm               # single-shot LLM answer
CODE>rubee bee --llm=       # single-shot with a specific model

Contributing

If you are interested in contributing to ru.Bee, please read the Contributing guide.
Feel free to open an issue if you spot one.
Have an idea or want to discuss something? Open a discussion.
